section: Chapter 5: The Fundamental Unit of Life

concepts.lesson: A cell is the smallest unit of life capable of performing all vital functions. All living organisms are made up of one or more cells. Cells act as the basic structural and functional units of life.
concepts.real_example: The human body is made up of billions of cells that perform different functions.

concepts.lesson: Cells were first discovered by Robert Hooke in 1665 when he observed cork under a microscope. He noticed small box-like structures and called them cells.
concepts.real_example: Robert Hooke observed cork slices under a microscope.

concepts.lesson: The cell theory states that all living organisms are composed of cells and that the cell is the basic unit of life. It also states that new cells arise from pre-existing cells.
concepts.real_example: Growth of living organisms occurs through cell division.
key_points: All organisms are made of cells
Cell is basic unit of life

concepts.lesson: The plasma membrane is the outermost covering of the cell. It separates the cell contents from the external environment and regulates the movement of substances in and out of the cell.
concepts.real_example: Oxygen enters and carbon dioxide leaves the cell through the plasma membrane.
key_points: Controls entry and exit of substances

concepts.lesson: Diffusion is the movement of substances from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration. It plays an important role in the exchange of gases in cells.
concepts.real_example: Oxygen enters cells and carbon dioxide leaves cells by diffusion.
key_points: Movement from high to low concentration

concepts.lesson: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration to lower water concentration.
concepts.real_example: Plant roots absorb water from soil through osmosis.

concepts.lesson: The cell wall is a rigid outer covering found in plant cells. It provides structural support and protection to the cell and is mainly composed of cellulose.
concepts.real_example: Plant cells maintain their shape because of the cell wall.

concepts.lesson: The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle that controls the activities of the cell. It contains genetic material called DNA organized into chromosomes.
concepts.real_example: The nucleus controls cell division and inheritance of traits.

concepts.lesson: Cytoplasm is the jelly-like fluid present inside the cell membrane. It contains cell organelles and is the site of many metabolic reactions.
concepts.real_example: Most cellular activities occur in the cytoplasm.

concepts.lesson: Cell organelles are specialized structures inside the cell that perform specific functions. Examples include mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes and plastids.
concepts.real_example: Mitochondria produce energy for the cell.

concepts.lesson: Mitochondria are known as the powerhouses of the cell because they produce energy in the form of ATP through cellular respiration.
concepts.real_example: Muscle cells have many mitochondria because they require more energy.

concepts.lesson: Plastids are organelles found in plant cells. Chloroplasts are plastids that contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis.
concepts.real_example: Leaves appear green due to chloroplasts.

concepts.lesson: Cell division is the process by which a parent cell divides to form new daughter cells. It helps in growth, repair and reproduction.
concepts.real_example: Growth in children occurs through continuous cell division.
key_points: Two main types: mitosis and meiosis
